speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This fifth conversation with Arthur Sundberg takes place at his home in Potlatch on August 7th, 1975. I've heard that that was that Corliss engine was really a monster.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, they had two Of them. They had one that drove the sawmill, and then they had one that drove the cleaning mill and. Of course, the the one that drove the sawmill was. Half again larger than the one that drove the planer, but the one that drove the sawmill. It. They kept it in place down there after it was taken out of service for a few years. And, oh, there was a lot of. Different discussions as to what to do with it. The. The old Ju scrap iron dealer. He, he wanted it for scrap because it was it was the highest quality scrap that you could possibly find. But he wanted to buy it for so much a ton on board a car. Well, management approached me on it and and I disturbed discouraged them on that because they wouldn't only give the engine away, but they lose money too, because it would take it cost more money to take it out of there than it would to.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: What they did for it, for scrap. So they'd just sit there, but then finally. Name Williams up here in Spokane. And he's a he's a brother to this Williams that makes this. Port diamond temperature signs. You know you've seen these electric signs that change every minute and gives you the temperatures. He talked him into giving it to him. But he would take it out. That is he would pay for taking it out. And he was going to put it up, up and Spokane. And he's out there by Medical Lake, along with a lot of other old sawmill, mining and farm equipment and make a museum, you know, for the public. So they give it to him and… so he, he… approached me, wanted me to take it out. That was after I had retired. And I told him, no way would I take it out because I said, I, I don't do any lifting or slugging or any of that heavy work. Well, he said he didn't want me to do any work. He said he'd get ironworkers from Spokane do the work, but he wanted me to supervise it, telling him how to take it apart and and then mark all the pieces so they could be reassembled.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: So I said I agreed to that. So I supervised the taking the engine down and and shipping that the Spokane. But… oh that thing… he had run… from the time the mill started and. I'm not entirely certain whether that was a brand new engine when it was installed here. You see, that was built by this Minneapolis. Company. And there was only. Seems to me like there was only three engine that was ever built in that size, and the other two, I believe, was installed in some big breweries back in. In Wisconsin. I think that one in Minneapolis and I think the one in Wisconsin… but… the time that that was dismantled, they had it. Oh, they they disconnected the big steam pipe. There was a 12 inch steam line that supplied the engine, but they disconnected that and just hooked up a two inch airline to it, and there wasn't any load on it, you know. So.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Periodically when they had visitors coming through a bunch of visitors, then they'd start up the engine and let them see the run. It just run on on air pressure, you see, but no load. But that being run by a dual watch and said. Well, a person really should have a picture of it to see the. Get some idea of the size of it. You know, the, the flywheel on it was. 60in across and 24ft in diameter. And this folks on that wheel, you know, the spokes were about like so, you know, of course they weren't round. They were, you know, flattened with all cast iron. The frame was all cast iron. And. The only thing that was, was steel was the connecting rod. And of course, the bearings were, were bronze bearings with Babbitt inserts. But it had a 48 inch cylinder 48in in diameter. The cylinder, just one cylinder. And.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Get the stroke on it. But anyway, that was. 1200 horsepower in one engine, so it was quite a piece of machinery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you explain briefly how.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Corliss engine?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, it's a little bit difficult to explain… if… unless a person is is acquainted with, you know, with engines, stationary engines… this… i could, I could make you just a small sketch of the operating mechanism. Anyway, there was eccentrics on the main crankshaft. Between the the main bearing and the flywheel. There was two big eccentrics on the on the shaft there. Now, if you know what an eccentric is, that's it's something that runs off center. You seem like so. But then connected to these eccentrics was push rods, steel push rods. And these rods are about 20ft long. And they run back to the steam chest. And of course they worked on the outside of the steam chest a little. On the stage at the end of these push rods… they worked… an eccentric there, of course, it on the steam chest. But this, this rock back and forth like so. You see one at each end of this cylinder. And then there was a rod that come down from that down, and they opened and closed valves. Now they had rotary valves that went through. There was.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: An intake and exhaust valve on each end of the cylinder. And these, these rods would open and close these little rebels, you see, so that when the intake valve was opened on one end, the exhaust valve had to be opened on the opposite end and then vice versa, you see. And that pushed the cylinder piston back and forth in the cylinders. How was the steam generated? Well, from the power plant they they had a battery of boilers there. They had… they had 12… return flue boilers. And then they had one of course, this, this big water tube boilers wasn't installed until 1920. Beginning in 1925… but anyway they… they burned all their waste from the mill. Not all of it, but they, they burned whatever waste they needed from the mill to generate the steam. And and then. Surplus waste went into a big burner. This burner was.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I don't know the exact dimension, but about 60ft in diameter. And I think it was 110ft tall with the screen top on all the surplus sleds and sawdust and stuff that they didn't need that went into this burner, the corner. That burner. It was torn down. It was in the 1940s. I can't give you the exact year. But anyway, they they quit burning their, their surplus and. And and they hogged all that, their fuel. That is all the slabs went through a big hog knife hog that chopped it all up into small pieces, you know, and then whatever wasn't burned in the power plant it they put it in a conveyor system that went across to a big fuel pile where they could store this fuel, where they could bring it back in and burn it on. Weekends, holidays and times whenever the mill was running, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the engine provided the power for the entire song.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Everything. Everything that is entire sawmill. Plus the green chain… the… it was all driven. I think I told you here before about the the belt that used on that engine that went from the engine over into the sawmill… the… claim that the took 3000 cow hides to make that one belly. Of course. Now when you see a cowhide, they in making it belt, they don't use the entire hide because the flank and some of those parts of the hide are too soft. You see, they just use the backs and. They used mostly South American cowhide because they said that they were thicker in the I suppose it was done in a warm climate, you know… but… this thing was all put together with glue… and… whenever, whenever this belt would stretch, you know, which it would, you know, after put on a new belt, then it would run about a year. By then it had stretched to the point where they'd have to take a piece out of it and read it. And and.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: It takes an experienced man to do that kind of work. Each layer in thickness of this belt has to be separated back for a distance of about three feet on each end of the belt, you know where to cut. And then. Each each ply has to be separated back. And then these plays have to be shaved down to a feather edge, you see. And and then they had to be. Covered with blue. And then these plays would go together like so. And then they had great big wooden clamps that they, they clamped on that and they'd stay in these clamps until that blue was dry. And to make that joint so that smooth so that it wouldn't pound as it went over the pulley, you know, you take on the driven end, the small pulley way, it could make a quite a racket, you know. And it could, it could put a lot of strain on the pulley itself and on the burdens on the shaft.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know, if it if it had found every time he'd come over and of course, the speed did it go over by, it'd be just like a hammer striking, you know, if it wasn't good and smooth. So it was it was skilled at work, you know, putting the belt like that together. Well, then it's kind of hard to, to someone that picture. The transmission of power in the mill like that. Unless you could look at some drawings or sketches or something and get something idea. Then this shaft that supported the the main line shaft in the sawmill where the engine drove on to this main line shaft, it was seven inches in diameter. And and then of course, it… it was probably… 60ft long, but on this shaft were several other pulleys. And the transmitted the power to other shafts, big long shafts. And they were these other shafts would transmit the power to the main band saws. You know, they had four band saws. So they, they had.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Two of them would, would drive off in one shaft. Well then from that secondary shaft, they was, it would drive over on to a third shaft. And of course each one would hit it progressed over, would be a little smaller in size. But anyway that. That mill was 120ft wide and about 340ft long. And when you'd stand on the floor and look up like that, it was just a maze of chefs and pulleys and belts over the entire area. But then you take. Say that the main main shaft run this way. Well, then there was a another line shaft that run diagonally to that at 90 degrees to that that drove the edges. Well, to get this power off in this shaft, to drive a shaft that's running 90 degrees to that, that had to be done through some gears. And those gears were they were called core gears. And these, these gears were.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: About… five feet in diameter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they were.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: A cast iron hub with slots in it. And these slots they put in wooden teeth. And these teeth had to be fitted and shaped so that they went in there, and then a key would be driven in to tighten that tooth into the into the casting seat. Then you see you had. Bevel gear running in this plane, another bevel gear running in this plane. And of course, they matched perfectly together so that you would transmit the power in one direction to the other. Well then to drive the the green chain… there was a… from this… main shaft there was another. Belt that go over on to another counter shaft. And then of course, you'd run in the same plane as this main shaft, but then the, the power that went out to the green chain, it had to run right angles to that. So there had to be another big core gear around that. When you see that would transmit the power out to the and it's really that stupendous piece of engineering.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You take you take engineering here today, you can set a machine down in any location and at any angle. It doesn't make any difference how a machine is set down on its base, because you would attach the electric motor right to it. And that had. But when you when you're going to run a sawmill as big as this one was it. In the earlier years and run it off in just one power source, that that was really a piece of engineering… that's something that… future generations will have no idea what ever what it was. It's too bad that I thought lots of times it. You know, if I had known that a lot of these blueprints were going to be destroyed. And just, you know, mutilated and finally just burn way had really been something, you know, for person could have kept a lot of imprints.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was firing the engine. Was that a much to that? Was it a big deal that it would have to be done very carefully? Was it.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You mean the boilers?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, yeah.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You see the engine and you take the the four head rigs in the sawmill. They were all propelled by steam power. And each one of those head rigs run by a 12 inch cast iron cylinder. And these cylinders were. About 42ft long. And of course, it had a valve on each end of that, you know, to run this piston back and forth and that in turn pulled that carriage back and forth on the track. You see that where they saw up the lumber? Well, in each each rig. Had a steam nigger, and that consisted of two cylinders, one eight inch cylinder and one ten inch cylinder. And that would. You've probably been in a sawmill, have you? Well, you've seen this thing come up out of the hole and turned the log over. Well, that bar comes down and then it branches out like this. Well, there's one cylinder that attaches to this end and one to that end. So of course, the the different manipulation of these two different cylinders can make this bar do whatever you want it to do up above here, you see, because if this one goes up and this one stays put, then that bar is going to go forward, see.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Or if this one goes up the bar is going to go back. They both go up, it's going to come straight up. But anyway, there was one set like that for each, each. Henry. Well then there was a 12 inch cylinder about. Two foot stroke or each loader that in turn tipped the log off and logged onto the carriage. You see. Now that was just for, for each each great glen where the logs come into the mill… they had… 12 inch cellars there for to kick the logs out of one bull chain over on to the next chain, and then as it go in there they had another cylinder down below. Here they kicked the log onto each log de each carried. The carriage had its own deck of logs, you know, they had. So they kicked them up. So you see there were steam cylinders all throughout the mill. And all away from, from six inch in diameter and 12 inch stroke to 12 inch diameter and so on. So it took an awful lot of steam. Well, then, of course, they furnished the steam for part of the town.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The mercantile store and the hotel and the school. And the big church that burned down… as well as… five houses upon novel, where the brass was all heated with steam from the mill. But then when they started to build dry hills, the first dry killed, I think I told you before was was an experimental deal that nobody in the lumber industry knew anything about drying lumber with dry kills until. The latter in the latter part of the period when was involved in that First World War and in the of course, during wartime, the demand for lumber went up. So I and all that took too long to drive just air dry. So they started experimenting with drying it with steam. So then that took… more steam. But… they had another water room at the planer that drove that Corliss engine. And of course, that that boiler room, he's the one that furnished Dean for this first dry kill that they had. So the one bought a room at the sawmill. It just furnished steam for for the sawmill and for their pumps and for the town. Part of the town.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, then… in 19 2425… they'd… got into the… point where they they knew quite a bit about, drying lumber. So they built 24 big dry kills. I think I explained those gills to you before. But anyway, they at that time… they… connected the steam power line… and… at that time is when they put in this big water tube boilers, the big Kidwell boiler. And it was. Well, I'm not I'm not going to quote exactly but. I think it was about 1200 horsepower boiler just this one border. Well they used that then with these other four banks of butters that they had in the original power plant. Now those. Original boilers, they were. About five feet in diameter and. 20ft long, something like that. And they were made up in batteries of three boilers side by side in each battery. And they had four batteries, so there was 12 with those borders. You know, that was the original set of and.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They were returned fluid boilers where the, the water was on the outside of the flues, and the fire from the firebox went to the back end of the firebox and then come up and come through these flues and then out the stack, you see… and… these fires, these boilers, they were fired by Dutch ovens… instead of… well, the fire was partly under the boiler, but just, just the back end of the firebox was under the water. But anyway, the firebox was bricked over to where there was no fire, come in contact with the outer shell of the boiler. And then, of course, they had this conveyor system that run from the sawmill through the boiler room. That carried the fuel. It was the sawdust and ground up slabs, you know, and then for for each boiler they had a metal spout that come down this conveyor system run quite high. It was probably.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Ten feet above the deck of the boiler room, and they had metal spouts that opened on top, just metal troughs that come down into this round fire hole. It was just a casting. It was opening of about about 12 14 inch opening. And in the bottom of this conveyor they had slide gates that was operated with a long handle that the firemen need walk along. He looked down in these holes and see how he's fires were, you know, and of course, wouldn't take long to learn just how much fuel that you are supposed to keep in this firebox. There, you see, because you're not supposed to have any exposed grates. And he could adjust these levers on these to adjust the amount of fuel that come down, you see… well, now… prior to 19… early part 1925… when they put in this extra big boiler and built these dry kills.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: A man working in the powerhouse, the firemen. They really had a job cut out for him, but still it was something attractive about it to where it was for years and years there that that was 12 hours a day and and seven days a week firing these boilers. And the only time there was ever an opening for vacancy, there was if somebody had to move out of the country or died, you know. But you take during the working hours of the mill, well, these firemen, they could just, as I say, just these slides and let the fuel run in there, see. But then they also had to let some fuel run out onto the deck. And this deck was. Oh. About ten foot wide back from the the spouse come down back to the wall. Well, they could run fuel onto this deck, but then when it got up there to where it wouldn't run out anymore, then these men had to go up and shovel it back up against this wall, you know, bank up as much as they could so as to have enough fuel in there on this deck to run them the hours that the mill wasn't running, you know, like on a Sunday or holiday or or on the.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Two hours during the night, you know, when it wasn't running. Well, at that time, they had to keep these fires going. They had to maintain this steam pressure. So they had to shovel it in there. They had these big barley forks and they would shovel this fuel in that. They'd stored there during the day into the whole sea. So that run into a lot of hard work. But then after 1925, when they put in this big boiler. They also built a big fuel house so they didn't have to store anymore that fuel on this deck because all the surplus fuel, then wood would go spill over into a conveyor from this powerhouse conveyor that went to the right angles up into a fuel house, and that was a long conveyor that would distribute that in there. Well, then, whenever they needed fuel, rather than when the sawmill was running, well, then one man would go out in the fuel house and one man.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Would stay in the powerhouse. There'd be two in the power. There was three men on the ship, the water tender, and two firemen. But this one man had got in the power house, and he would start that conveyor down there. And then they had doors that would open. Full length of the building was a whole series of doors each. Each door was about or six foot wide and they could open them up in this fuel, had run into this conveyor and go back up into the powerhouse and circulate around. And of course, whenever the fuel got low in the in this fuel bend, and the man out there had to do quite a bit of breaking down and shoveling to get at the end of that conveyor. So making steam was more or less complicated, and it took a lot of manpower and a lot of hard work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Talking about the power being supplied town, it makes me think of what I was told that the town would not only be part much, you know, supply of power in the town, but that the town functions were all like the schools and and different things like that were run by management. Is that.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That's right. It was it was a closed school district here. And it was just for the town. And. There was no one outside the company that paid any school taxes. The company paid the entire cost. They they furnished the buildings. They paid all the. Administrative expenses of the school. So everything was free to the people, to the kids. And. We talked about Mr. Laird before and all during his lifetime here he was always at the head of the school board, and he was stickler for education… this pilot school… as long as long as it was administered that way, it had the… highest reading… in later county and the highest reading in the whole state of Idaho. In standing. Because this Potlatch school board, which was partly lumber company, they just hired the very best teachers that could be found. And they maintained a high degree of education as far as they went. You know, the grade school plus the high school. So they they really had an outstanding, outstanding.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: School system here all during that period. I don't know, I think possibly that. What I'm thinking about probably would carry you to the entire nation. But, you know, it used to be that. Every kid in school was taught penmanship. And all all the subjects that they that they did carry. The kids were really brilliant. And you never every subject you'd take write today. The average kid that that finishes high school and I might say probably everyone that finishes college. You've got to have a kind of vivid imagination if you're going to even read their name. You know, they can't write. Kids use typewriters nowadays. You know they can't, right? And they can't figure they've got to use a calculator if they're going to do any figuring. But it always wasn't that way before they got all these gadgets because, you know, they the kids were taught penmanship. That was one thing that was pounded into every kid. And.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Another thing too, he was every kid was really outstanding. And being able to read and to write and to figure. You know, addition, subtraction and multiplication and all those things. They were really sharp at it. Then maybe, maybe I've got a wrong picture now. But what kids that I've seen in the last few years, they can't even figure out chains for $0.50.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I don't know. I've heard that Potlatch had had an outstanding school system. But I also heard that they divided up the the management of the town among the different managers of the superintendents of the company, and each one had a certain responsibilities for, for running the town. And that struck me as an odd kind of a way as far as the local people being involved in, in managing a town like you take a town like Troy, where you know, where there's a city council and it's all run by and by the local people. And what I was thinking about, about the schools and, and this was.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, not that that had a discouraged local people.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, no, no, it it didn't discourage them at all because you take everything, everything was run so efficiently that. Unless it be some individual crackpot, did you find some that was figures. Well, he could do it better, but, you know, a company town is something that was really unique. There wasn't too many of them in the in the whole United States. You found more of them, I suppose, back in the mining. Districts in the in the east were the coal mine operator company. They they owned these shacks and they owned a company store. And but you take away those towns were run was absolutely no no comparison at all of what he. This town was run and of course I, I don't know just how many company towns were in the lumber industry, but I know of one more. I'm McLeod, California. There was a probably still is I'm not sure. Wholly owned company town and sawmill operation. It was a big operation. They owned all the houses and the hotel and everything.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But McLeod didn't have. Quite, quite as good a set up as they had here, because I've been in McLeod and and they never maintained the company down there and never maintained their sidewalks in the streets as well as they did here. And they never maintained their houses as good as they did here. I don't believe. And. They didn't have quite as many. Public facilities, you know, in a way of for churches and gymnasiums and stuff like that. Here. They had everything. Here they had the. Well, to start with here they had what they called the Union Church, and that was non-denominational. See, that was all denominations, you know, wanted to get together. Well, then. There was so many Scandinavians, Swedes and Norwegians, you know, that they there. State church over in the country where they come from was Lutheran. So then they they wanted church of the company build a Lutheran church. Well, then there was.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Quite a few Catholics or build Erie. The first general manager was a Catholic, so they built a Catholic church. So they had three churches besides the. Grade school, which went through one through eight grade. And then they had the high school from nine through 12… and… then this big gymnasium that I believe I told you about before. And hotels and boarding houses and, and. Building that was housed the after the got to so that they had moving pictures. Why they of course they come the owned the building. Theater itself was privately run, but they had just about everything here that you would find in any small town, you know, confectionery and. Pool hall and just, you know, everything that you would find anywhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In terms of, of self-government or governing the town itself, regulating. What about the involvement of local people in that? I mean, besides the brass, did the brass really take 95% of that, of that running onto themselves because the company owned the town and.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They took 100%. They took it all the people, they didn't have any part in it. That is in the management of the town… he… the town itself, the the administrator of the town was was usually the. Head bookkeeper in the office. Or auditor or whatever you want to call him. Well, then they had a what might be classified as a town superintendent. And he was the one that that looked after all the details of of the town, that is, I'm talking about the the physical structure of the town. If anybody had if you had a leaky water faucet or if you. Thought that your house needed redecorating on the inside, you go down and see him. And of course, if you had a leaky water foster, he'd send the plumber up and have it fixed. And if there was a hole in your sidewalk where they'd send the carpenter up and repair that. They had wooden sidewalks all over the town. And.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Then they had a. Pretty good, fair schedule of about redecorating the houses on the inside and possibly painting the outside every five years. But in the case of somebody moving out of a house and the wallpaper, the paint or something being in bad shape, why, they'd send the crew in and refinish the inside of the house before the next tenant come in and dig for the the amount of rent that was charged. Now this house, this is a three bedroom house. Well, when we moved into this house, we paid $20 a month rent. You know, you can't own a house for that, you know? And then, as I say, anything that needed repairing while they repaired it, they paid the taxes. You didn't have no taxes to pay, or you didn't have any school tax to pay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of vantage do you think this this gave the company? Do you think it was really a profitable thing for them?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, it wasn't profitable. No. But, they owned everything to begin with. And then they kept the ownership and they kept up the maintenance of everything up to the time when all top management, you know, you get up in the board of directors and president of the company and all that sort of thing. You know, they have the meeting and they discuss these things… and finally,… gosh, I forget what year we didn't buy this house. But anyway, when they sold these houses, now, this is a pretty nice street that we're on here. In fact, the houses on this street were built in 1925. So you see, they're quite a bit newer than the rest of the town. That is a majority of the town… and… there's three houses on this street here, this one and the one across the street and one down at the end of the street, where all of the same floor plan. And.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: We bought these houses for $3,250. And all the rest of the houses on this street. There's a lot of nice houses here. Sold for $2,600. So you see, they didn't make any money on that at all. There was people on this street here that, that they would if if they wanted to, you could buy one of these houses for 10% down. And then monthly payments on the purchase of it. So you see, on a $2,600 house, a 10% down payment was only $260. And I know there's one family lived here that finally got around to make it. Greeted by their house, they made a down payment and they probably didn't make over 2 or 3 monthly payments until they sold out for $6,000. They see, as far as the company making money is concerned, they didn't make any money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you think they're thinking was on on maintaining the company town in the in the early years. I mean, we've talked about the fact that that let's say in the men that they were promoting to become a higher ups, they were looking for production. That's what they wanted. So it's hard for me to believe that they did that. They maintained this town as a company town and put into it what they did for for just altruistic.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I know, but then you've got to look at it this way too, that. They undoubtedly figured at the beginning there that it would take so many dwellings to support a certain number of people that would be working for them to produce what they had to produce. But if they didn't have control of these dwellings. Then possibly they would be a big percentage of these dwellings would be occupied by people that didn't work for them. Consequently, then the people that did work for them wouldn't have any place to live. So you see, they they always had every time a new family come in, they always had a house for them. You could come in today and go down to the office and ask for a house, and it may not be the house that you'd want, but you'd have a house to move into tomorrow, and possibly in a month or 2 or 6 months, or sometime in the future, they would find a house for you, you know, more to your liking.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And that way, you know when they had control of all these houses, why, they could keep the people satisfied and happy. And oh, I know that the time that people were paying rent, there were lots of them had done a lot of bellyaching about paying rent and money. So down the drain and all that. But some of these same people, when they got around to where they bought their home, then they found out how cheap it was to rent once he. Because when you had to pay for the maintenance of a house, pay your taxes and your insurance. Why? Well, it takes a lot of money just to own home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think that that the attitude of most of the. Well, I imagine there are different attitudes. And I'm thinking about ways that that people work and felt about the company, whether they thought that that, They should get whatever they could from the company or whether they really, let's say, appreciated and felt the company was doing a lot for them.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I'll tell you, you take the. You might say, the class of people that might be the wrong words to use because I don't know as class enters into it too much. But anyway, you take people that are used to living. In this sort of an environment, you know, they just take things for granted, you know, and they don't don't think about a lot of the details. They really didn't. I don't think the average person really thought much of it. It was just a case of having a house to live in and having a job and reporting on the job at 7:00 in the morning and and having a one hour for noon and then finish out the day and, and then in the evening. Why they could spend their time leisure time anyway, they wanted to you see, they didn't do much thinking about that… it was… the average young person that is, I say, man that is working here, that that was single. He would probably make plans for the weekend, you know, or and.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: There wasn't too much I don't believe that. But they really thought about outside of just making a living for their family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess why I ask it is because the company town and that that way of of having a town is so different than, than, you know, 99% of the towns in America. So it seems like a as far as the the way the town was handled and run there, there would be real differences if the person knows that if, let's say, I've heard of, oh, somebody who's whose husband died when she had to leave the town because she she wasn't… no.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I don't think that that wasn't true at all. It wasn't true. She might leave the town, but she didn't have to leave the town. She could stay here and and try to make a living in a way that she could. Now, she might if she wasn't trained, she might have to make a living by taking in washing or or going out and doing housework. And there was, there was several that had done that, but there was no such thing that they had to leave the town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would happen if the guy got fired by the company of each day?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, that is nonetheless he of course, if he got fired in one department and he could get just go over to the next department and hire on. See. And if he did, well, as long as he was on the payroll, he could work here, he could live here. But I don't think anybody I don't know of anybody that was actually kicked out because they were out of a job. If they could, if they could show that they could pay their rent and their expenses, well, then of course they go down and pay that in cash. You know, they could stay on. It wasn't it wasn't that rigid. You didn't lose your house just because you was out of a job, because that being out of a job might be just temporary. There might have been a few cases, you know, throughout the years where it was somebody was canned. And the reason for it was, you know, so severe that they just didn't want them in the town any longer, and then they might be asked to move out.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But those those cases were… all… a few and far between that I don't believe. It's hardly worth mentioning. You know, you take as far as taking the having the people take an interest in, in running the town. Do you have any idea at all how the people feel right today? You can canvass the people in this town, or the city of Moscow, or Pullman or Lewiston or Spokane or any place. And you find the big majority, not not just the majority, but the big majority. They don't know straight up, and they don't care a thing about it one way or the other. They just go along and just with any way the ball bounces. You know, the the average person in the average town, they can't tell you the name of the councilman. Probably a lot of them can't tell you the name of the mayor if they have a mayor. And. About all they can do is they bag guys, you know, I pay too much taxes. You'd say, well, how much do you pay? Well, I don't know. They don't even know that. What proportion do you pay in taxes to the school district? What proportion goes to the county? What proportion goes to the city? They don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think it was bad back then?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, they didn't have that to pay. Not in this town. They didn't have. They didn't have any of that to be concerned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't mean that. But then they're running the town.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But then you take for the country wide. Why there was always just a few individuals in every town, regardless of the size of the town, whether it be Potlatch or New York City. It was just a very few individuals in proportion to the population, that wanted to meddle with politics in the running of the city, but that that proportion in regard to to the population was a darn small. It probably wouldn't be less than 1%, you know. So people, as a rule, they're not they're not too concerned about details, you know, they're you know, they'll. Watch the television and listen to radio and get these commentators views on some of them things, and then they'll say, yeah, boy, the country's going to pot, you know, and but they haven't got any solution to offer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think? When the company was providing services for the town, for the people that that they really put aside? Saving money as a consideration? Did they try to cut corners wherever they could, or were they really.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Know the, the the management running the town? I'm, I'm thinking about well, I've heard in the, in the woods that they, that they watch every penny that they could. Well and I'm wondering whether in the town.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well don't you think that don't you think that holds true with running any company.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah. But the difference here is running the town. I mean, I see that as a I'm not, you know, that's not the same to me as running the company. Maybe it was the same.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, I know, but then in running the town, you know, if you or anyone else was responsible for the running of the town, the management of it, you would at least try to break him and even, wouldn't you, you know, if he was going to go in the red year after year. Well, then. When the top brass looked over the books, they'd say, well, that man is not the man for the job. So they get another administrator. So. It it wasn't the case of making money, but then it's just more of a case of breaking even.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With these responsibilities for the town. We handle this as a part of the of management's daily work. Or was that extracurricular?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, when you say management, of course, management spreads out, you know, from the from the top clear down to something that's. Well, almost to the point of being obscured, but still, it could be management, you know, now, as I say, the management of the town itself. Was run separately from the management of the sawmill, although the the general manager, he was the manager over all of it. But. These different departments, you know, they all had, you know, different department heads. And I say the, the town itself. Well, the. The auditor or the head bookkeeper, whatever you want to call him in the office usually was the administrator of the town. But he had what you might classify as a town superintendent under him. And he didn't concern himself with anything else except the the physical part of the town, you know, the maintenance and upkeep of the town. This town superintendent, he didn't have anything to do with renting houses because the auditor, he handled the rentals of houses. But as far as the maintenance of streets, sidewalks and all buildings, whether it be public buildings or private buildings, everything was was all handled by the superintendent of the town.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But he was might consider as part of the brass, you know. But as far as his salary was concerned, his salary probably didn't amount to. And he just a few dollars more than one of the men that was working under him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know whether they broke even in, in many, much of the management.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Of the. Well, of course, I, I never had access to any of the books, you know, and, I've sat in on a lot of meetings, you know, management meetings for the. Unit manager would would give us a lot of figures, you know, but whether or not they were true or not, I wouldn't have any way of knowing, because I of course, there was a couple of times there when he offered to let some of us look at the books because he thought that maybe by the look on our faces that maybe we doubted what he was telling us, you know… but of course, we'd never would take him up on that. But,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind what kinds of things would be would those be where you might doubt what you know?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, that that would be more or less overall. Cost of operation and an income. You know, from the from the mill and the town, the whole the whole layout. And, you know, it's only, I guess, natural that the larger the operation or then people think that the more the province, you know, you take a small operation, people naturally think it's small province, a large operation, naturally think it's arts profits. Well, that true in some instances, I guess. And there was a time here. Oh, you know when. After you got going again, after the depression that we had in the 30s. Well, management would explain to us that. That all they were doing is trading an old dollar for a new dollar… and it wasn't making a dime. All they were doing was just trying to get a dollar back for a dollar spent and without any profits. And you know that during that period when he offered, let some of us look at the books. But,… take as far as their they own an awful lot of Timberland and.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You take when lumber was selling for. Horse, they take an average price. They just roughly speaking, say an average price of $50 a thousand. And they they had a valuation set on their books as to what the timber was worth standing. Well, then you had the cost of logging, transportation, manufacture, sales and finally get it to the consumer. By that $50 average that they was getting cost them $50. And in some cases it cost them more than that. So you see, it's you get to figuring finances in on big scale. And there's so many things that the general public don't know a thing about. You know, all they can see is they're shipping millions of feet of lumber and they're getting thousands of dollars per thousand feet of lumber and all of their getting all that. And look at all the money. Nowadays, they are making a lot of money, there's no question about it. But there was for good many years that they didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think then that probably it was a dollar for dollar during.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That period? Yeah. And that was they had to manage awful close in order to get that. Of course you take modern technology is building up now to where you take you probably heard the expression about the packing houses, the slaughterhouses, you know, they use everything but the squeal. Well, that's the way it is. And the lumber industry now, the only thing now that in the lumber industry in this region that that they don't use. Is the bark on the tree. But outside of that way, they use everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any responsibilities yourself? Besides your actual job when you were a, foreman for town activities?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No… no… of course, after the sold the town. Well, then, of course, the people in the town, they had, they had to elect councilmen and mayor. And now you take this little town. We don't. There isn't very many houses here. Small town. But still, now we have to maintain a. Full time policeman with an automobile. He. I don't think they get too much salary. But anyway, he probably gets $700 a month. Then they have a so-called town superintendent supposed to look after the streets. And one thing and other. He gets about the same salary. He's a policeman… and… of course, the mayor and the council, and they're they're not salaried. But then they have a town clerk and she draws a salary. Well, then you got the street lighting and. There's just an awful lot of overhead, and you divide that up. Then with the number of residents that we've got here and it comes pretty high.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Were taxed the highest. Of any community in later county were taxed higher than Moscow is for… in millage… and… so you see when the company had the town we didn't have any of that to pay. They paid everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what people felt when the town was going to be sold?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, everybody was really elated. They they thought that was something great to be able to own their home. And it is it. It's a nice feeling to own your home. But, you know, the first year that we owned this house, our taxes was $28. And now our taxes run close to $150… besides the… insurance, you take our our insurance we used to get that for. Three years for about $27. Now. Then it cost us $59 a year… and… then of course, it's,… i painted the outside of all of our buildings last year. And that's something, you know, that should be done every. Four or not more than five years. And so there is a lot of expense to owning your own home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would be the the reason that the company would have for kicking somebody out? What would be the sort of fence that would justify that? And they're, you know, just moving them out of town and their way of looking.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: At it. Oh… i suppose if he was proven to be a. Confirmed thief, you know. Determined to be stealing something from somebody or from the company all the time, or, or if they were just running a. Boisterous house, you know, something that disturbed the neighborhood. You know, there was you can imagine most any offense that was really to the point of being obnoxious. Well, then they might ask them to leave, you know, but any any petty offenses, they'd never do anything about that. They.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How effective was their attempts to control liquor in the town?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They didn't they didn't make any attempt to control the consumption of liquor. The only thing is that they just wouldn't permit it to be sold here. You know, they wouldn't allow any saloons in the town. But the men that worked here. They could bring it in by the suitcase load and and they could ship it in here by express. Now I have seen… these great big… steel express cars, you know, often the main line I have seen as high as three of those coupled together, switched on to this line here and moved in here, just all loaded with booze. Most of it would be. 50 gallon barrels of wine, you know, for the Italian community. But there was whiskey was shipped in here every week by express, you know, in small packages. But that was just for the individuals consumption, not for resale. Or I suppose if they if I send up to Spokane and got four quarts of whiskey and you wanted one of them, I'd certainly one. There was nothing to be said about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So really they they didn't. Keep us out of town at all. Just they just, discourage the public gathering. That's right. In the saloons and created the kinds of fights that we talked about back in the Midwest. What about the the, arm of the law? Did the, they.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The company.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pretty strict order as far as that?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah, they, I, I think possibly that they had the, the best relationship here between the people and the law of any town in the whole United States. You know, the. This town superintendent at that time was also the constable. And, of course, he was deputized as a constable, you know, from through the sheriff of the county. But of course, the company paid all of his his salary… and… the only time that he ever put anyone in jail would be if. Somebody would swear out a complaint. Then of course he would have to serve that. But generally he would, even if you would come to him with the complaint where he would try to talk you out of it. He was really a nice guy. And oh, I've seen it so darn many times that someone downtown and probably in the pool hall or confectionery and and drunkard and a doll and probably couldn't even stand up.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Or maybe he was just drunk and wanting to fight, like some of them get. Well, maybe the proprietor would send for old Harry Gleave, the constable, and he'd come and. Talk to the guy a little bit, and then pretty soon he'd look around and say, well, you're a pretty good friend to him, why don't you take him home? That'd be the end of it. That's the way it was always run. That is, up until after they sold the town. Well, then, of course, then they had to have policemen and deputy sheriffs and patrol cars and boy, they they just imagine, I guess that crime really got bad after the town was sold and incorporated. But before that, they didn't need all them law enforcement people. Of course, now, with all these beer parlors they got all over the country. Boy, it really takes a lot of law enforcement because they.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I guess they really stir up a mess. Every day, every night and every weekend, you know, and need beer Pirates. I can't tell you too much about that because I never go in one of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If two recent it matter anyway.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, it's,… you take you take people that act like that is just disgusting. Revolting to me. I just don't want to be around them at all. I've got nothing against drinking. Is a person wants to drink. That's fine. Go ahead. But then getting, you know, acting the way they do when they get out into one of them places. Now, they wouldn't act like that if they could consume the same amount, you know, in, in their own home or in your home or in a little party. But when they get into when they're beer parties, then they really have to whoop it up. And I guess from what I've been told, the women are ten times worse than the men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you about the depression here and, what happened? When are it? Pulled the pin?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, of course… you would have to make some of these. Responses to that over a period of time. You know, it wasn't that quick. You know, when they decided to shut the plant down for the lack of. Business. Well, they didn't notify the people. They sent out notices that the plant was going down. And of course, they notified everybody that was on the job, but they also notified everybody that. That they could live in the house and there would be some work they couldn't promise how much work or what it would be, but it would be some work for a few people. And whatever work there was to be had, it would be prorated so as to help as many people as possible. And for a community. I believe that we fared better here than the average community anywhere in the whole country… there was… a few that had.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: A lot of initiative that, you know, would get out and try to improvise. And then, of course, there was a few that like, you'd find any place, I guess it would just sit on their hands and cry and didn't know what was going to happen. But then they all weathered it anyway. But at that time. Most everybody put in a garden. The company furnished all the ground that you could possibly want for to put in a garden, and they they'd give you any help that they could, you know, for putting it in the garden and. We were, we were part of one little gardening deal. It wasn't really cooperative in all respects. It was that we all went together and higher demand to plow up this piece of ground. And of course, this piece of ground was each one was would draw his own plot as the ground he wanted to work. But then we, as far as working the ground is concerned why we all went together and shared the the cost of working it up.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But then each one of us then planted their own garden and cultivated and harvested our own garden. So that helped a whole lot. And. Oh, I can tell you just from our own experience. For a time there we bought cream from a farmer out here in the country. He'd bring in about three of these eight gallon cream cans of sweet cream a week into the station here, and whatever they paid him per can for the two cans, he'd sell them. We'd pay him that for the one. Can you tell us he. Well, of course, eight gallons of cream would be too much for us. So we then in turn would divide that. And my wife's sister and brother in law would take half of it and we'd take half of it. Well, we'd used part of the cream, you know, for cake, you know, ice cream cake or put it on cereal or and then part of it, we churn into butter.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Made her own butter. Well, it was that butter cost us about $0.18 a pound. Then there was a farmer over here. All of that come through down here peddling. And he had cracked wheat, and he was telling about how good that was for cereal… so I bought… like about $0.15 worth. Gosh, I got enough there to ruin us for a year. Wheat was only selling for. Two bits of bushel… well… then I stocked up on. They had a special on this. Maybe rice, you know, coming packages, you know. So I stocked up on the. Oh, gosh, I don't know how much of that. Enough to run us for a year or so… and,… then we bought flour about four barrel of flour at a time that was 1,650 pound sacks… and… we got a good price on that. I don't know, I guess the sack of flour was. Somewhere around a dollar. I can't tell you the exact price.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Then in our garden… but we… put up about 20 sacks of spuds and we had several sacks of carrots and buggies, and we had lots of cabbage and everything, so we had lots of food… well… i went down here at the mill and I made up a few butcher knives. And then I traded some of these knives to a farmer out here for a half a hog… and… you know, you can do a lot of things, you know, if it was necessary… as far as far as any hardships is concerned, we never knew what hardships were at all. We had more food to eat than we ever had in our life. And and,… pretty near might say just about every Saturday night. Be a a dance up here in this Legion cabin. And and it would be just. Oh, just anybody that wanted to sponsored it really didn't cost him anything to sponsor, because all you do is probably be downtown in the middle of the week and.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Say to meet somebody and said and talk and say, well, let's have a dance Saturday night up the cabin. Okay. So they talk it up and send that word out. Ladies. They'd bring a few cookies or a few sandwiches and. Left some coffee and. Then the music they had the by boys. There was a bunch of brothers. They had an orchestra and they'd play for whatever they could get. They didn't have any fixed fee. Well, if you wanted to drop dime in the hat or nothing at all, or two bits, anything you wanted, you know, that you felt that you wanted to give, put it in the hat. And that's what the music got… everybody… socialized and… fraternize and had a good time. Everybody, you know, wasn't the same boat. And but then just as soon as times picked up again and people started getting some money, well, then all this fraternizing kind of died out. Then it divided up into small groups. You'd have three friends and I'd have 2 or 3 friends, and this way, in that way.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But during that toughest part of the depression, why they were all together in a, in a group.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was more fraternizing during the depression than there had been before it.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder if that's why many people, when they remember the depression, don't think of it as such a hard time because they they think of the closeness of the people at that time.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah. Well, of course, that depends a whole lot on who you're talking to. And, in the area in which you're talking about,… take the people that really suffered during the depression was the people in the cities, because in the cities there's no fraternizing at all. And and in the cities, why? Well, I know when they started them. C.C. camps. Well, they started sending out a, an awful lot of these young men from in the eastern part of the country out this way… well, they had several CC camps up here in the timbered areas. And,… i was in the dentist office down here one day and just visiting with the dentist, and he would laugh. And you tell me that they brought a bunch of these cc's in, and of course, they was under the control of the Army. They had a real tough army, sergeant. You know, it was a in every camp. And he had to be tough to in order to get along with some of them guys.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But anyway… i guess I don't know the Army doctors or what looked them over. But anyway, they of course there was a lot of them needed some dental work done. And so they brought 3 or 4 truckloads down here, you know, give them a little recreation. And of course, there would be several of them there that had to go into the dentist and get some work done. And there's one of these young guys come in and sit down the dental chair there. And he had his foot all bandaged up. And Dennis asked him, he said, what'd you do with your foot? What happened? Well, he said to tell you. You know, he said, back where we come from. He said, we heard a lot about this wild West out here in Indians and and everything. And so we got out here. And so some of them, they was telling us that we had to watch out for these wild animals. Of course, we said we didn't know what they look like. And so they were clearing brush, clearing out an area for the camp, and mostly just a small brush. And of course they give them an ax piece.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And they were chopping down these bushes and, and I guess the bushes were falling down around his feet, you know. And finally he said he'd seen something wiggled down in there, you know, and he thought that was one of them wild beasts. And he let fly with the action. He chopped his own foot. But it was full of here by the name of Ivan Ross. He was a carpenter that worked down here at the plant for a long time, and he. He hired on as a as a camp carpenter when he was building these camps. And he had some men and and he had to construct these, bottoms for these tents. You know, they built a floor and sidewalls on it and then, of course, canvas stuff. And he said, clearing these areas. He said there were some of them guys that they had the issue, hand saws for them for cutting this little, little bitty brush, you know, and just he said they didn't dare give him an ax. He said they were chopping himself and chopping each other and everything else.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They didn't know what an ax was. So to give them hands and cut them little bushes off, you know. Well, anyway, I, I was working in the, in the powerhouse and. They was pro rating the. Labor in the powerhouse there. So that's make it go as far as it could. So I was working for hours a day and I think about four days a week getting in about 16 hours. But anyway, I was working powerhouse there when a bunch of these CCS come down there and they had somebody with them for a guide and was showing them the plant. Of course, the plant was all down, but then they could come in and look at this big engine and they could look at these fires and that sort of thing, and they could see anything else on the plant they was interested in. But they was mostly young men. Above average intelligence, you know, because they showed interest in something like that. So I was talking with one young fella there. He was a big tall on a.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Pretty good looking young fella. And so I asked him where he was from, and he said he was from New York. So I said, well, what did you do in New York? What did you work at before you come out here? He looked at me kind of funny and I said, did you have a job? No. Well, I said, where'd you get by? He said, just as long as he could remember from the time he was born, all he ever knew was just to go down and get in a super line every day. And worked their way up to where they can get the chunk of bread and bowl of soup. That's all he knew all his life, until he came out here and he was growing up, man. So it was really tough back in, in the big cities. Of course, he was growing up, and he was in that soup line long before the depression hit, you know, and his he was probably full maybe 19, 20 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to these men who didn't have jobs? Let's say the guys have worked on the saws, people who didn't have a job where they could just, work in a power plant or something that was necessary to maintain the mill. They didn't have any work. Or did the company make work for them?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They made work for for some of them, but they couldn't make work for all of them. Now there was. They started a WPA. They had those projects started after Roosevelt got in. And. So this highway from Moscow going towards, Court Lane… most of that highway was built at that time with WPA labor and… it was more or less hand labor. They had some machinery, but they had used a lot of horses and Fresno's and slip scrapers and. And the men that worked on that highway, they were same way. They got just four hours a day for so many days a week, you know. So spread it over many good. And. Most everybody had some work of some kind. You couldn't be choosy on on the work. If a job was offered to you, you better take it. Rather, if it'd be dirty, use the Dickens or anything else… and… one project that the company made was put a new roof on this water reservoir for the town, right up here at the end of this street. And it's a big concrete reservoir, holds about a million gallons of water. But the roof covers a pretty big area. Well, they reroofed that. And of course, the company furnished the material. Of course the company owned it.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But for carpenters on the job, carpenters got paid $0.27 an hour. And that's what we got. Paid down at the mill. $0.27 an hour. Well, I sure I a far cry from what they're getting now, but. Of course, if man earned a dollar, why, he could buy it quite a bit without a dollar. You know, you could buy… oh, for… half of that, you could buy a pretty nice piece of meat… and… spuds would be about $0.50. Oh, all things in, in proportion way. People weathered the storm pretty well. Of course, there was nobody that made any money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the company had to make some pretty hard choices about who they would be able to support and who not?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, it was it was the one one thing that they did do. They, they definitely wanted to keep all what might be considered key men, you know, here. They didn't want them to get away. You take men of, of skill in the production of lumber. You take. The filers in in the sawmill that filed the saws, the sawyers and the sawmill. Setters, lumber graders and some of their top mechanics. They all they wanted all of them to stay, and they all wanted to provide them enough work so that they would stay. Now you take this base labor, common labor. They was down at the end of the totem pole. Because if and when the time comes, when they'd get to operating again by. Those jobs could be a lot easier than these jobs that required a lot of skill, you see. But even those just common labor, I don't know of any of them that.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But what they got some work somewhere. You know, they if they didn't work here, why they probably worked on the road or someplace, you know, and put in there. Everybody cut their own wood those days. Why we all burned wood in their. Kitchen ranges and in our heating stoves. And we could get all the timber we wanted for nothing. Well, we had to do was cut it and we had to first we had to provide transportation for hauling it home. But I know there was. A lot of cooperation there too, even in making wood. Most, most everybody that cut wood, they would cut it in four foot lengths and hall at home. And fill it up. Well, then there was old 2 or 3 year, possibly more individuals that had buzz saws, no automobile. They converted over into a wood cutting outfit, you know, took the body off from it, and they just had the running gear and, and they'd got to get Ahold of a saw harbor and a saw and, and so whenever they go to cut, somebody would buy that person that owned the wood.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: He would get some of his friends and neighbors come over and help. Well… you know… it wasn't too big a trick, you know, to cut 40 cords of wood a day… because… one man or two men bringing the wood up to the saw, and they generally moved that saw to kept, kept it right up to the woodpile. One man actually sawing the wood and another man off bearing, you know, taking these chunks as they come off the saw and throwing them out of the way, they… they'd probably cut… ten cord for you and come over and cut maybe ten cord for me and maybe ten cord for somebody else in, in a day. But, the only one that got any pay was a man that owned the saw. He got so much a cord for cutting the wood. The rest of the help is all cooperative. One would help the other when you see. But generally in making the wood out in the woods. Why?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Usually it was a one man deal there. Sometimes two would go together, you know, and if they were using a crosscut soul, then two men would work together and you know. And they'd divide the wood that they made. But there wasn't any power source in the woods. It was all crosscut saws.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the company give preference to married men over single men? As far as.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, well… they did, except if there was some single men that were held jobs of, you know, of importance, then of course, that single man, he was given the same consideration. But they were pretty fair about keeping families, you know, together. I, I think they'd done a real good job of administering the whole thing, after all. You know, it was pretty difficult times for everybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you were going to add it up and look at the advantages and disadvantages of a of a company town, how do you think they they would way out? I mean, I'm not saying that, on balance it's it's gonna be terrific or it's gonna be bad, but, I mean, we're in a good. What do you think would be the advantages and what would be disadvantages of it?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, you know, somebody listening to these tapes, if they ever do in the future, and you put a question like that to me about the advantages and disadvantages of company town, you've got to take into account, there's a lot of differences. It might be a lot of differences in a company town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I guess I have to think of this.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: One, but thinking of this one here… all you take… i think I think in, in every aspect of the, of the town why the people were better off as a company town than they are as an open town, because of the fact that. The company maintained and they did it, done a good job of maintaining all the facilities and all the buildings and and everything, and there was no added expense to any of the people that lived here. They paid. When a person rented a house… well… house rent run from about $8 a month up and I guess probably the top rent for some of the best houses in town. Probably run up to maybe a $35 a month… but when you stop and consider that,… the streets, sidewalks, the buildings inside and out, the schools, the churches. Everything was maintained and paid for entirely by the company. And you didn't have to pay for any of that. You was bound to be better off in the company town, and you was in owning it yourself. The only thing is that.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: When a person gets to retirement age. Well, even when you're retired, I have to pay the same taxes and same insurance and same maintenance cost as a person would 35 years old. There's no difference. I pay the same taxes as as anybody else would. This paying taxes for the valuation… so,… money wise, I. You're bound to lose money by owning it. The only thing is, you've got something there. An equity that probably if you ever did want to sell, you'd have you'd have something to sell, which you wouldn't have if you was renting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Company town is any drawbacks as compared to a non company town. Or were there any drawbacks at Potlatch that you can see?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well I can't, I can't see any for for this town. With the exception that if a person. Say wanted to start some private enterprise. If you wanted to start a grocery store or. Or another confectionery or or something that was already here, you couldn't do it in this town because they wouldn't give you a permit to do it. See? If you wanted to start up something that wasn't already here, then fine, you could do that. But they didn't. They protected those that was in business by not allowing any competition in that business in this town. But you could just go over the hill here, just a quarter of a mile to Onaway. And that was that was an open town. And they had a grocery store there. And, and they would run competition with a grocery store here. You know, some people like to trade over there and some didn't. But that's a that's about the only disadvantage that I can see to Company Town is that they control all private enterprise too in the town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Back in the old days, there used to be some people who were critical of it just because it was a company town who would say.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh yeah, yeah, I think, I think you'd find that, but. I think if you'd try to analyze it, the same people would be critical of any system in any town in the country, regardless of what it was. Some people are just natural born. Grapes and want to bellyache about something all the time. And that's, you know, it's just a just a matter of human psychology. You can if you could canvas this town or Moscow or Troy or any other town right now. If you could just. Well, if you could get somebody to sit down on a bench in that Freedom Square in Moscow right now and talk with you and talk with you for an hour, and then you'd get somebody else talk with you for an hour on the same subject, in the same sequence of subjects. And you do that for eight people for eight hours or an hour apiece. You'd probably get eight different pictures of what you want to talk about.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Some of them would be happy with everything and some of them would. There wasn't. Not one single thing in the city of Moscow was right. The town council was all wrong. The mayor, he should be shot. The sewer systems are no good. The water systems no good. You can't drink the water because it's putrid. Anything. I don't care what it is, but that's just human psychology.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think for the company town. Looking at it in in the past, the reason that it wouldn't have a good name would be because of things like what happened in the East and in places like Pullman, you know, where where it was a company town. And the dissatisfaction of the the workers was so great that it led to the, you know, severe labor disturbances.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, and riots and that I think that that's where I would see the source of that.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I don't think I don't think that held entirely true with company towns in the East either. They're, they're big riots and, and troubles was. Trying to organize labor. I guess that's not here, but why they're putting a rug down on their porch over here, I think. But… you take… the Pullman Railroad Car Company, you know, and the steel mills and the coal miners, they fought a awful hard battle for years, you know. But it was most of those people outside of the coal miners lived in. In their own homes or in houses that they were running from individuals. And it wasn't a company home, but their troubles was. All on being able to organize labor. And these operators, they spent millions of dollars and trying to prevent the organization of labor. But then they they finally did went out on the organization of labor. And then when they did, they they created a monster that, that they can't control anymore either. Labor has taken control of the world.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Labor is is, is much more corrupt than management ever thought of being. Here? They're looking for Jimmy Hoffa right now. They don't know where he is, but it's just a matter of involvement in organized labor. He's trying to take over the Teamsters union again. So there is no I don't think there's any really cut and dried answer on any of these things. It's it's just a matter of more or less a personal opinion. And. But I think if you just wanted to break it down in regard to a couple of town. Bring it right down just to. The fundamentals and they get down to the nitty gritty. The old thing is that I would have to say that they were all right. They were they had a lot of advantages. Just for the fact that. In those days, the average person didn't come into a town with the. In here very much money in his pocket. He might have lots of kids, but no money. And in company town.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: If he was hired and he got a job, he was given a house and. He could get fuel delivered to his house and that would be taken out of his paycheck. He didn't have to have money to get it. He could pay for it by working, and he could establish credit at the store to where he could buy food and clothing for his family, and he could get that on credit. He could pay for that as he earned it. So you take from the standpoint of a person. That's in that category, economically, it was really a blessing. It was he had everything that he needed for to sustain him and his family with, and he got it with just the idea that he would work for it. And that's that's something that, that you couldn't get in other enterprise. Now you take at the same period of time. If a man come into Spokane, say, with his family and he come in there and he was broke, but he went to out to one of these sawmills, or he went to the slaughter house, or he went to any other business established in Spokane and asked for a job and was given a job. Well, then it'd be up to him to.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Ram around town, looking for a place where he could find a place to live. And he'd probably find some old broken down apartment building, or some old house that was ready to fall down. But the owner of that property, he wanted cash in advance for rent before he could move in there. Then he would go to the store to buy some groceries or buy some clothing for his kids, and they wanted cash before he could get it. So you see the advantage of the company town over private enterprise when you're looking at it from the point of of a person in that particular category. Now, in the early days, that fit for everybody. There was very, very few people that. That really saved up anything. Those that did save for people that were well established and had been there for a long time. But ordinarily, the ordinary person that was moving into the area.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They didn't have much when they got their.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'd like to ask you something more about what you're just saying, because before when we talked, you said that, it was very hard for most people to get ahead. It just get by. And that's what it's been, what they earned. Well, was there see, I'm wondering about why that was. Why if you if you stay and work in a place for years, you don't, you know, or whether now you're what you're saying now is that when you if you stayed for long enough in a place and, and got well enough established, you would get into cumulate so some money.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well you dig if you just if you just stop and and figure. Now say a man gets a job and his income for the month would be, say, $70, that's his total income for the month. But. That $70, he has to pay a few dollars for rent. He has to buy his his groceries by clothing. And in some cases, he would probably make a small payment on whatever furniture he had to buy each month. And then, of course. In most cases, why they would probably use a little bit of money for for recreation. Might want to go to the baseball game or and then, another thing too is that the. I think the majority. Of the families at that period of time by the man of the house, he probably drank a lot of booze. He spent quite a little bit of money for booze. And I know that the, the people that, that I knew and was in contact with the time that I was growing up, that's very much held, very much true.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And it was generally the woman in the house that really had managed to to keep food on the table and keep her kids clothed and ready for school and all that sort of thing, because a man would work hard and he would come home and he'd eat and pile into bed, and next morning he'd get up and go to work.
